scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific law enacted by Solon for the Athenian state.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Solon's legislation is traditionally dated to his archonship in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text identifies this as one of the democratic features of the constitution of Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This reform was a core part of Solon's legislative program in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle attributes the right to claim redress for others to the constitutional reforms of Solon.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This legal innovation is dated to Solon's archonship in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The provision for appealing to jury-courts is cited as a pillar of Solon's Athenian constitution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The institution of appeal was established by Solon in -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes an Athenian compromise following the expulsion of the archon Damasias.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The fall of Damasias and the subsequent board of ten archons occurred around -580.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The revision of the citizen-roll occurred in Athens immediately following the fall of the Peisistratid tyranny.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The tyranny of Hippias was overthrown in -510, leading to the immediate revision of the rolls.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The loans are described as a specific policy of the tyrant Pisistratus in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Pisistratus established his final and most stable period of rule starting around -546.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This agricultural tax was a revenue measure implemented by the Athenian tyrant Pisistratus.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This tax was a feature of Pisistratus's established tyranny beginning in -546.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The institution of local justices was an administrative reform by Pisistratus in Attica.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This judicial reform is dated to the stable period of Pisistratus's tyranny beginning in -546.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle quotes an 'ancestral statute' of the Athenians specifically regarding tyranny.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: While called 'ancestral', it is cited in the context of the struggle against tyranny ending in -510.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The expulsion of the 700 families was carried out by the Spartan Cleomenes within the Athenian territory.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Cleomenes' intervention in Athens and the expulsion of the families occurred in -508.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This change in official identification was a central component of Cleisthenes' democratic reforms in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Cleisthenes introduced his major constitutional reforms in -508.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This protection of religious rites was a specific provision within Cleisthenes' reforms of the Athenian tribes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Ostracism was a uniquely Athenian legal mechanism used to handle political leaders.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The first actual ostracism (of Hipparchus) took place in -487, as Aristotle notes.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes a specific geographic restriction added to the Athenian law on ostracism.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This amendment was passed just before the Persian invasion at the time of the recall of the ostracized in -480.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle describes the state-funded maintenance of citizens during the height of the Athenian Empire.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This system of widespread public pay reached its peak around -450 during the leadership of Pericles.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This was a legislative change to the eligibility for the Athenian archonship.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This specific citizenship law was a motion passed by Pericles in the Athenian assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Pericles' citizenship law was enacted in the archonship of Antidotus, which was -451.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle attributes the introduction of jury pay to Pericles in the Athenian democratic system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Pericles introduced jury pay during his political ascendancy, approximately -450.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule was established by the revolutionary oligarchic council during the coup of the Four Hundred in Athens.
